stem: If $p(x) = (x + 3)(x - 2) - (x + 1)(x - 4)$, which of the following is equivalent to $p(x)$?
explanation:
Expand each product using FOIL, then subtract.

Step 1: Expand $(x+3)(x-2)$:
$$(x+3)(x-2) = x^2 - 2x + 3x - 6 = x^2 + x - 6$$

Step 2: Expand $(x+1)(x-4)$:
$$(x+1)(x-4) = x^2 - 4x + x - 4 = x^2 - 3x - 4$$

Step 3: Subtract, distributing the negative sign to every term of the second expression:
$$p(x) = (x^2 + x - 6) - (x^2 - 3x - 4)$$
$$= x^2 + x - 6 - x^2 + 3x + 4$$

Step 4: Combine like terms:
$$= (x^2 - x^2) + (x + 3x) + (-6 + 4) = 0 + 4x + (-2) = 4x - 2$$

distractorRationale.A: Correct. Distributing the negative sign to all three terms of $(x^2 - 3x - 4)$ gives $-x^2 + 3x + 4$, leading to $4x - 2$.
distractorRationale.B: Partial sign error. A student who correctly distributes the negative to the constant term ($-4$ becomes $+4$) but fails to flip the sign of the $x$ term ($-3x$ stays as $-3x$ instead of becoming $+3x$) would compute $x + (-3x) = -2x$ and $-6 + 4 = -2$, giving $-2x - 2$.
distractorRationale.C: Partial sign error. A student who correctly distributes the negative to $-3x$ (getting $+3x$) but fails to flip the sign of the constant $-4$ (it stays as $-4$ instead of becoming $+4$) would compute $x + 3x = 4x$ and $-6 + (-4) = -10$, giving $4x - 10$.
distractorRationale.D: Complete failure to distribute the negative. A student who subtracts only the $x^2$ term and leaves all other terms of $(x^2 - 3x - 4)$ with their original signs would compute $x + (-3x) = -2x$ and $-6 + (-4) = -10$, giving $-2x - 10$.
